extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box that houses a heatbed, an internal fan, two temperature sensors monitoring the air inside the box and one sensor monitoring the ambient temperature outside. The plant is driven by a Raspberry Pi that runs a bang‑bang‑type controller (with lower/upper temperature limits, heating duration H and waiting duration C) to regulate the temperature regardless of the content placed inside. The fan is used to enforce the assumption of uniform temperature distribution inside the box. This simple CPS represents a temperature‑regulation use case suitable for Digital Twin experimentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (1) real‑time visualization of incubator status by ingesting controller and low‑level driver messages into a time‑series database and displaying them on a dashboard; (2) on‑demand calibration of the thermal models (Model B) via an automated calibration routine; (3) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman filter that fuses measured sensor data with Model B predictions; (4) what‑if simulations through co‑simulation that leverage historical or live PT data to optimise controller parameters; and (5) a self‑adaptation loop that detects plant behaviour changes, schedules data‑gathering experiments, re‑calibrates models, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and finally executes the updated control strategy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation relies on RabbitMQ as the communication server/message broker that connects the Raspberry Pi controller, the low‑level driver and DT components. A low‑level driver abstracts sensor and actuator access and publishes temperature readings to RabbitMQ. The controller code runs on the Raspberry Pi and implements the bang‑bang logic. Calibration algorithms and the Kalman filter are executed as DT services. Data are stored in a time‑series database (unspecified) for dashboard visualisation. Open‑source hardware and software specifications are hosted in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator). Simulation models (Model A and Model B) are used for temperature prediction, and co‑simulation capabilities are employed for what‑if analysis.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The incubator Digital Twin is orchestrated around a central RabbitMQ message broker (AMQP) that synchronises communication between the physical twin’s low‑level driver, the Raspberry‑Pi controller, and the DT services. The low‑level driver reads temperature sensors and publishes measurements to RabbitMQ; the controller subscribes to these messages, issues fan and heat‑bed commands, and also publishes its state. DT components (real‑time visualisation, calibration service, Kalman‑filter based anomaly detection, what‑if co‑simulation, and the self‑adaptation loop) subscribe to the same topics, consume the data, update model parameters, run monitors, and publish results back through the broker. Calibration algorithms periodically update model parameters (Model A, Model B) which are then used by monitoring and control optimisation services. Thus the system follows a component‑based, message‑driven architecture with the broker acting as the coordination hub and resource manager for all DT services.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through RabbitMQ message exchanges and a time‑series database that stores sensor streams for dashboards. The DT listens to controller and driver messages (AMQP) and forwards processed data to the database for real‑time visualisation. Co‑simulation for what‑if scenarios can ingest historical or live PT data via the same message channel, allowing external simulation tools to be coupled. No additional enterprise‑level standards (e.g., OPC‑UA) are mentioned; the integration relies on open‑source components (RabbitMQ, time‑series DB, dashboards) and the publicly available GitHub repository for hardware/software artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator Digital Twin incorporates two analytical temperature dynamics models (Model A and Model B) derived from the heat‑balance equation Q = c m ΔT. Model A uses all simplifying assumptions (uniform temperature, negligible wall heat storage, constant air specific heat, all electrical power converted to heat) while Model B relaxes the assumption that the heatbed does not absorb heat, giving it four free parameters and a higher fidelity for predicting heat‑bed thermal inertia. Sensor data are the primary data source: two internal temperature sensors and one external sensor provide time‑stamped temperature readings. The low‑level driver periodically reads these sensors, wraps each measurement in a RabbitMQ message and publishes it to the communication server. The controller and the DT components subscribe to the same RabbitMQ broker, ingest the data into a time‑series database, and feed it to calibration algorithms that estimate the free parameters of the models. The calibrated models are then used by monitors, a Kalman‑filter based state estimator, and what‑if simulation co‑simulations. Model relationships are explicit: raw sensor data → calibration → updated Model A/Model B → monitors/estimators → optimisation algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the incubator is provided by a resistive heatbed (thermal actuator) and an internal fan (air‑flow actuator). The heatbed is switched on/off and driven for a programmable heating duration (parameter H) to raise the box temperature; the fan is turned on to homogenise the air temperature. Actuation commands are issued by the Raspberry‑Pi controller, which implements a bang‑bang control strategy with lower and upper temperature limits (LL, UL) and a waiting interval (C) after each heating pulse to avoid overshoot. Commands are transmitted as RabbitMQ messages to the low‑level driver, which forwards them to the physical devices. A safe‑protocol embedded in the low‑level driver guarantees that commands keep the plant in a safe state; the controller also respects the waiting duration to limit temperature excursions. All actuation is addressed remotely via the message‑based interface rather than direct hardware pins.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is realised with three thermometric sensors: two are placed inside the insulated styrofoam box to monitor the air temperature that the controller regulates, and one sensor is mounted outside the box to record ambient (room) temperature. The low‑level driver periodically reads each sensor, packages the measurement into a RabbitMQ message and publishes it to the communication server. The published data are subsequently stored in a time‑series database for visualisation and model input. The documents do not specify sampling frequency, data‑rate, or sensor accuracy, so those details are not available.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by comparing the predictions of Model A (two free parameters) and Model B (four free parameters) against experimental temperature recordings; experiments demonstrated that Model B more accurately captures the thermal inertia of the heatbed. Fidelity uncertainties stem from the simplifying assumptions (uniform temperature distribution, negligible wall heat storage, constant air specific heat, and complete conversion of electrical power to heat) listed in the modelling section. On‑demand calibration algorithms periodically re‑estimate model parameters using the latest sensor data, reducing parametric error. Validation is performed through on‑line anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman filter that fuses Model B with measured temperatures, enabling detection of deviations caused by modelling inaccuracies or unforeseen disturbances. Calibration results are visualised (e.g., Figure 13) to assess model‑parameter convergence, and the self‑adaptation loop re‑calibrates models whenever significant anomalies are detected.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data are periodically read by the low‑level driver from the three temperature sensors (two inside the incubator, one outside) and published as messages to the RabbitMQ communication server. The messages contain raw temperature measurements (°C) and are sent using the AMQP protocol supported by RabbitMQ. The driver runs a safe protocol that checks command messages before sending data, providing basic quality assurance. DT components subscribe to the same RabbitMQ topics to receive the data for real‑time visualisation, calibration and anomaly detection.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for the heat‑bed and the fan are generated by the controller (a bang‑bang controller with parameters LL, UL, H, C) and sent as RabbitMQ messages to the low‑level driver. The driver validates the commands against its safe protocol before forwarding them to the hardware actuators, then confirms execution back to the controller. The DT can also inject or modify commands by publishing to the same RabbitMQ queues, enabling a closed‑loop self‑adaptation flow.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All cyber‑physical communication is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker using the AMQP protocol. The controller runs on a Raspberry Pi and connects to RabbitMQ over the local network (wired or Wi‑Fi). The topology is a star/bus where each component (controller, low‑level driver, DT modules) is a client of the broker. RabbitMQ provides reliable message delivery, persistence, and can be deployed on‑premise or in the cloud, offering redundancy and fault tolerance. No explicit bandwidth, latency, or security protocol details are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The controller software executes on an on‑board Raspberry Pi (edge device). The RabbitMQ broker and DT services (visualisation dashboard, calibration engine, Kalman filter, what‑if simulation) can be hosted either on‑premise or in a cloud environment, as RabbitMQ is described as easy to deploy in both contexts. Time‑series data are stored in a database for dashboarding. Specific computational, storage, scalability or availability specifications are not detailed in the supplied documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The incubator Digital Twin lifecycle described in the documents proceeds through (1) initial hardware and software setup of the physical twin (styrofoam box, heat‑bed, fan, temperature sensors, Raspberry‑Pi controller); (2) development of two simulation models (Model A based on four assumptions, Model B relaxing assumption 4) and their implementation; (3) calibration of the models using calibration algorithms that estimate parameters from sensor data; (4) online monitoring where data from the low‑level driver are streamed via RabbitMQ to the DT; (5) anomaly detection and state estimation (Kalman filter) that feed back into the DT; (6) what‑if simulations and optimisation of controller parameters; and (7) a self‑adaptation loop that re‑configures the controller and re‑calibrates the model when the plant behaviour changes. Transition between stages is managed by the calibration process and the self‑adaptation loop, and the lifecycle is governed by continuous monitoring and re‑calibration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process combines open‑source hardware (Raspberry Pi), RabbitMQ message broker for communication, a low‑level driver that reads sensors and forwards messages, and a Bang‑Bang‑style controller that reacts to temperature limits. Models are built offline, calibrated on demand (section 3.3) and then deployed in the DT. Quality assurance is implicit in the calibration step and in the anomaly‑detection Kalman filter that validates sensor data against model predictions. The DT evolves through iterative cycles: data acquisition → model calibration → monitoring → anomaly detection → experiment scheduling → data‑driven re‑calibration → controller re‑configuration. Version control or explicit testing frameworks are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT generates insights through real‑time visualization of temperature trends, on‑demand calibration results, and anomaly detection using a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature. What‑if simulations allow prediction of system behaviour under alternative controller settings, supporting optimisation of heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). The self‑adaptation loop decides when to trigger experiments, recalibrate Model B, adjust the Kalman filter parameters, and re‑configure the controller, thus closing the decision‑making loop. Recommendations are based on model‑based predictions and are presented via a dashboard.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The incubator Digital Twin incorporates safety measures through a 'safe protocol' embedded in the low‑level driver, which ensures the physical device remains in a safe state while interacting with the Physical Twin. Communication between the controller and the low‑level driver is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker, providing synchronized, reliable message exchange for temperature readings, control commands, and state updates. The controller on the Raspberry Pi implements a bang‑bang control strategy with defined upper and lower temperature limits (UL, LL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C), and it periodically receives temperature measurements before issuing actuation commands. Although no explicit authentication, access‑control, or encryption mechanisms are described, the combination of the safe driver protocol and the controlled message flow via RabbitMQ constitutes the documented safety and security considerations for this DT system.

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.

twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. ## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
